texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recent years have seen rapid improvement of technology and large-scale applications of lithium-ion batteries, which leads to an increasing market demand for lithium. Since the land lithium resources are diminishing drastically, the sources of lithium extraction have shifted to the large amount of water resources containing salt-lake brines and seawater. Among the varieties of aqueous recovery approaches, membrane technology seems to have huge development potential and good application prospect. This is because the membrane technologies exhibit excellent Li/Mg separation selectivity, with low energy consumption and green process owing to no addition of chemicals. The present work reviews the latest advances in various membrane technologies, including nanofi ltration membrane, electrodialysis, membrane capacitive deionization approaches, solid electrolyte electrolysis-based technology, etc. The recent developments in positively charged nanofi ltration membrane are discussed in terms of the preparation methods, membrane properties, and Li/Mg separation coef fi cient. In addition, the effects of several factors on electrodialysis for lithium extraction and relevant mechanisms in both simple and actual saline systems are discussed, including applied voltage, VC/VD, and coexisting ions. The applications of electrodialysis with novel selective membrane involving nanofi ltration membrane as well as solid electrolyte membrane and perspectives for further investigation are proposed.

texts.text: Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lithium is the lightest element and has several favorable characteristics, such as electrochemical activity, the highest redox potential value, and the highest speci fi c heat capacity of any solid element [1 -4]. And its compounds have wide applications in various areas,involvingnuclearfusion,pharmaceutical,ceramicsandspecial glass, lubricants and greases, primary aluminum production, rocket propellants, synthesis of organic compounds, silver solders, lithium battery and lithium-ion battery batteries [5 -10]. Especially in modern society, electric vehicles, and electronic products such as cellular phones and laptop computer, go through a huge revolution, calling for the great demand for lithium ion batteries (LIBs) [11,12]. Moreover, in the future, hybrid and electric vehicles as well as light vehicles would potentially be powered by lightweight LIBs as a result of the high charge-to-weight ratio of lithium and share an increasing number of market proportion [13 -17]. Therefore, there would be a strong market demand for lithium and the lithium extraction is signi fi cantly important.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In general, lithium occurs in two major resources: the Li-containing igneous rocks, such as Lepidolite, Spodumene, Petalite, and Amblygonite, and Li-containing water resources, such as salt-lake brine and seawater [18 -21]. At present, the former is commonly exploited while the development of the latter by industries is not very mature. Nevertheless, it is reported that lithium in brines and seawater accounts for more than 60% of the total lithium amount and concentrated in countries including Chile, China, Bolivia, Argentina, and the USA [18,22 -24]. Hence, it is worthwhile to develop an ef fi cient lithium extraction technology for lithium recovery from aqueous sources.
texts.text: In general, lithium occurs in two major resources: the Li-containing igneous rocks, such as Lepidolite, Spodumene, Petalite, and Amblygonite, and Li-containing water resources, such as salt-lake brine and seawater [18 -21]. At present, the former is commonly exploited while the development of the latter by industries is not very mature. Nevertheless, it is reported that lithium in brines and seawater accounts for more than 60% of the total lithium amount and concentrated in countries including Chile, China, Bolivia, Argentina, and the USA [18,22 -24]. Hence, it is worthwhile to develop an ef fi cient lithium extraction technology for lithium recovery from aqueous sources.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To date, several approaches have been developed for recovering lithium from salt-lake brines and seawater, such as precipitation [25 -28], adsorption [29 -32], solvent extraction [8,33,34], ion sieves [35 -37], nanofi ltration [38 -40], electrodialysis [41 -43], etc. The lime-soda evaporation process is the most commonly used approach, containing several procedures to remove magnesium and precipitate Li2CO3 [44]. However, the evaporation process takes a longer time of 12 -18 months and several chemical wastes are produced. Another precipitation approach with a great deal of research is aluminate precipitation method [27,29]. During this process, aluminum salts as AlCl3 and caustic alkali as NaOH are used to produce amorphous Al(OH)3, which could react with brine to precipitate Li in the form of LiCl  2Al(OH)3  xH2O. But this method consumes a large amount of the caustic alkali and thus is not costeffective. Additionally, solvent extraction has attracted a great attention for recovering lithium [33,34,45]. Various chelating agents and ionic liquids are developed and better results could be obtained in both low and high Mg/Li mass ratio brines. However, organic extraction agents could probably cause damage to the environment, especially in brines located at Qinghai-Tibet plateau, China. Although the ion sieve is an effective method for lithium extraction from brines and seawater and has several advantages as low energy consumption and environmentally-friendly features [32,46]. Its poor virtual Li uptake capacity restricts practical industrial application.

But this method consumes a large amount of the caustic alkali and thus is not costeffective. Additionally, solvent extraction has attracted a great attention for recovering lithium [33,34,45]. Various chelating agents and ionic liquids are developed and better results could be obtained in both low and high Mg/Li mass ratio brines. However, organic extraction agents could probably cause damage to the environment, especially in brines located at Qinghai-Tibet plateau, China. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane technology is comprised of various selective membranes with unique structures capable of separating Li + from Mg 2+ in brines and seawater. It provides an effective approach for lithium extraction from aqueous solutions containing multiple coexisting ions (Na + , K + , Ca 2+ , Mg 2+ , etc.) [43,47]. High Li purity and recovery could be obtained as a result of the high Li separation ef fi ciency. Moreover, this technology consumes lower energy and conforms to the green principle, with no need for additional puri fi cation in the industrial operation. In the past decade, many researches have been conducted on the lithium extraction using membrane technology, such as nanofi ltration, electrodialysis, membrane distillation, etc. However, industrial applications have gone through a slow development as a result of the complexity of actual brines and seawater system, especially those with high Mg/Li mass ratio. In recent years, researchers have done experiments using various feed characteristics and provide fundamental and useful information for lithium recovery in actual systems in the future [48 -50].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this review, various membrane technologies developed for Li/Mg separation, the separation mechanisms of lithium from magnesium, and the preparation method, properties, as well as the performance of membranes will be discussed. Moreover, emphasis is laid on the Li recovery rate, separation ef fi ciency of Li from Mg, energy consumption, and green principle. Additionally, the factors in fl uencing the separation performance of membranes, such as temperature, pH, feed volume, operating pressure, applied voltage, and coexisting ions, are discussed. Finally, some prospects concerning membrane technology for lithium extraction are highlighted.

texts.orig: Membrane technique is one of the fastest growing and fascinating separation technologies [51 -54]. Although the membrane technique is a relatively new-type separation approach, it is proved to be extremely effective in terms of molecular and ionic levels. In the past decades, several pressuredriven membrane technologies, i.e. nanofi ltration (NF), reverse osmosis (RO), forward osmosis (FO), membrane distillation (MD), and electric fi eld driven membrane technology as electrodialysis (ED), have extensive applications for water treatment, such as desalination, water softening and separation, and concentration of solutes [39,55 -58].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: NF is a pressure driven membrane separation technology with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) ranging from 200 to 1000 Da and has a separation capability for monoand multivalent ions with low operating pressures and operating cost [61 -65]. Among the various membrane technologies, NF has the most widespread applications in lithium extraction, including negatively and positively charged nanofi ltration membranes such as DK membrane [66], NF90 membrane [48], EDTA modifi ed NF membrane [59], MPMC high- fl ux positively charged NF membrane [38], etc. The properties and separation performance of various NF membranes are shown in Table 1.

texts.orig: Negatively charged NF membrane
texts.text: Negatively charged NF membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Up to now, most commercial NF membranes are fabricated using aliphatic amine monomer and show negatively charged surfaces as a result of the presence of sulfonic and carboxylic groups in the polyamide layer [68]. The common negatively charged nanofi ltration membranes used for lithium extraction are a series of NF90 membrane. Somrani, Hamzaoui and Pontie [39] studied the separation performance of NF90-2540 membrane in the diluted Chott Djerid (Tunisia) brine (Mg/Li mass ratio of 56.76). This membrane showed the Mg 2+ rejection rate of 100% and the Li + rejection rate of only 15% at low operating transmembrane pressure. However, in terms of the separation of Li + from Na + , unsatis fi ed result was obtained with the difference of Li + and Na + rejection rate of less than 15%. Moreover, compared to the pure water permeability of 15 L m � 2 h � 1 bar, there was a large decrease of permeability, with the diluted brine permeability of only 0.7 L m � 2 h � 1 bar, and thus membrane productivity suffered from a signi fi cant loss of 50% as a result of its fouling. Another commercial NF-90 membrane from Filmtec (DOW, USA) was investigated for lithium separation from magnesium [48]. The separation effect is unsatisfactory. It is shown that the Mg 2+ rejection, Li + rejection, and the separation coef fi cient were 60.5%, 15%, and 2.1, respectively, with the decrease of Mg/Li mass ratio from 20 to 9.3.

texts.orig: Additionally, a Desal nanofi ltration membrane (DL-2540) was utilized for the separation of lithium from magnesium in simulated East Taijiner brine (Mg 2+ /Li + mass ratio 44) and West Taijiner brine (Mg 2+ /Li + mass ratio 64) [67]. And several in fl uence factors were systematically investigated, such as the in fl ow water temperature, operating pressure, pH, and Mg 2+ /Li + mass ratio. It is indicated that the operating pressure, pH, and Mg 2+ /Li + mass ratio had an obvious impact on the separation performance of DL-2450 membrane. A high operating pressure and a low solution pH could help to increase the rejection rate of magnesium and thus bene fi ted the separation. However, higher Mg 2+ /Li + mass ratio had a negative effect on the separation. It would increase the rejection rate of lithium and decrease the rejection rate of magnesium. Meanwhile the permeate fl ux of membrane was decreased, which may be due to the concentration polarization phenomenon caused by the higher Mg 2+ concentration. Moreover, it could be found that the presence of some coexisted ions like Na + and K + was not conducive to the recovery of lithium ions. texts.label: text
texts.orig: As is known, NF membranes are designed and used to remove organic compounds and polyvalent salts and consume lower energy due to the low operating pressure [69]. Indeed, the negatively charged NF membranes are capable of rejecting Mg 2+ and permeating Li + . However, several impactors, such as solution concentration, Mg 2+ /Li + mass ratio, and membrane performance, limited the large-scale application of these negatively charged NF membranes. Since the membranes are easily fouled and the Li + suffers from a growing rejection with the increase of Mg 2+ /Li + mass ratio. Moreover, the negative surfaces of these NF membranes may have an adverse effect on the rejection of positive charged Mg 2+ as a result of the electrostatic interactions [70].

texts.orig: It is known that the selectively separation behavior of NF membrane is mainly governed by the Donnan exclusion theory [71 -73]. According to Donnan effect, the negatively charged NF membrane exhibits better rejection performance for anions [74,75]. However, the separation effect is unsatisfactory in the case of treating solutes with positive charge [38]. Therefore, positively charged NF membranes for lithium recovery attract much more attention.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 1. The atomic force microscope (AFM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), thermal gravity analysis (TGA) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) techniques were used to characterize the membrane. The AFM images and results of water contact angle showed relatively smooth surface and better hydrophilicity of MPMC membrane, probably attributing to the effective dispersion of M-CNTs and the interaction between MCNTs and PEI [76]. It is shown that the pure water fl ux of membrane increases from 20.8 L m � 2 h � 1 to 56.1 L m � 2 h � 1 . Meanwhile, the amino contained chains on M-CNTs could react with TMC to form polyamide and thus connect with PEI, which improved the salt rejection (MgCl2) of MPMC membrane, from 94.2% to 96.9%. Results obtained in the simulated solution containing 1.866 g/L MgCl2 and 0.134 g/L LiCl showed that, the rejection of MgCl2 and LiCl are approximately 95% and 18% respectively and the Mg/Li mass ratio decreased from 21.4 to 1.3. The slightly declined rejection of LiCl attributed to the low concentration in simulated solution. Owing to the reduction of the transmembrane pressure [76,77], the solution fl ux of MPMC membrane had a slight decrease to approximately 34 L m � 2 h � 1 .

The atomic force microscope (AFM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), thermal gravity analysis (TGA) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) techniques were used to characterize the membrane. The AFM images and results of water contact angle showed relatively smooth surface and better hydrophilicity of MPMC membrane, probably attributing to the effective dispersion of M-CNTs and the interaction between MCNTs and PEI [76]. It is shown that the pure water fl ux of membrane increases from 20.8 L m � 2 h � 1 to 56.1 L m � 2 h � 1 . Meanwhile, the amino contained chains on M-CNTs could react with TMC to form polyamide and thus connect with PEI, which improved the salt rejection (MgCl2) of MPMC membrane, from 94.2% to 96.9%. Results obtained in the simulated solution containing 1.866 g/L MgCl2 and 0.134 g/L LiCl showed that, the rejection of MgCl2 and LiCl are approximately 95% and 18% respectively and the Mg/Li mass ratio decreased from 21.4 to 1.3. The slightly declined rejection of LiCl attributed to the low concentration in simulated solution. Owing to the reduction of the transmembrane pressure [76,77], the solution fl ux of MPMC membrane had a slight decrease to approximately 34 L m � 2 h � 1 .

texts.orig: A similar technique was used to develop a positive charged nanofi ltration membrane with considerable protonated primary and secondary amine positive groups ( � � NH3 + and � � NH2 + ) [47]. ATR-FTIR, scanning electron microscope (SEM), AFM, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and zeta potential were conducted to characterize the chemical structure, morphology, surface and cross-section properties. It is illustrated in Fig. 2 that a polyamide selective layer, with a denser and rougher surface and without perceptible pores, was successfully formed on the surface of the PES substrate membrane. Zeta potential results showed that the PEI-TMC composite NF membrane had an isoelectric point of pH = 9.3 as a result of abundant -NH3 + and -NH2 + from PEI. And the rejections of Li + and Mg 2+ stabilized at approximately 19% and 95%, respectively during 3 times cycles, where Donnan exclusion theory dominated in the separation performance [78,79].

texts.orig: After modifi ed using the polyethyleneimine (PEI) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC), the PEI/TMC composite NF membrane exhibits great performance. Both salt rejection (MgCl2) and pure water fl ux are improved. Moreover, it shows better stability than the negatively charged NF membrane (NF90). As the Mg 2+ rejection of PEI/TMC composite NF membrane could remain at approximately 95% during 3 times cycles, making it have a great potential for Li + /Mg 2+ separation from brines and seawater. However, due to the positive charged surface of composite membrane and Donnan exclusion theory, the Li + rejection is a little bit higher and poses some challenges. Therefore, further studies should be conducted for decreasing the Li + rejection.

texts.orig: Besides, EDTA was used to modify the NF membrane prepared using branched poly ethylene imine (BPEI), TMC and crosslinked polyetherimide and thus a novel EDTA modifi ed NF membrane PAB-E was fabricated (see Fig. 3) [59]. It is proved that the amide bonding structures were generated and the separation selectivity of Li + against Mg 2+ could be greatly enhanced by using EDTAmodifi ed NF membranes PA-B-E, which may attribute to the adsorption of Mg 2+ by the EDTA-modifi ed surfaces. It showed better performance in the simulated brine (LiCl/MgCl2) with a Mg/ Li mass ratio of 24, with Li + rejection rate, Mg 2+ rejection rate, the separation factor SLi/Mg, and the fl ux being less than 30%, 92.3%, 9.2 and 6.0 L m � 2 h � 1 respectively, under the optimal condition. Moreover, the separation capacity for Li + and Mg 2+ remained stable within 36 h fi ltration. In addition, the rejection order of various single inorganic salts by PA-B-E was MgSO4 (91.7%) > MgCl2 (84.6%) > Na2SO4 (83.1%) > NaCl (69.1%) > LiCl (68.1%), which was different from the general positively charged membrane governed by Donnan exclusion (MgCl2 > MgSO4 > Na2SO4 > NaCl). This may be due to the existence of EDTA and the higher molecular weight of MgSO4 than MgCl2. Namely, this probably attributed to the synergy effect of Donnan exclusion and chelation of EDTA.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It could be seen from the fabrication process that the EDTA modifi ed composite NF membrane has similar structure with the PEI/TMC composite NF membrane mentioned above. But the EDTA modifi ed composite NF membrane exhibits excellent stability than the PEI/TMC composite NF membrane. This may attribute to synergy effect of Donnan exclusion and the chelation of EDTA. As the EDTA has a great af fi nity to combine with divalent cations and many studies have been conducted for removing divalent cations using EDTA functionalized materials [80,81]. However, it is reported that pH has a great in fl uence on the removal of divalent cations by EDTA functionalized materials [82,83]. Therefore, further research should be conducted on the effect of pH on the Li + /Mg 2+ separation using EDTA modifi ed composite NF membrane. Nonetheless, it could also be expected that the EDTA modifi ed composite NF membrane has a prospect application for Li + /Mg 2+ separation from brines and seawater with high Mg/Li mass ratio.

The schematic of the experimental NF set-up is shown in Fig. 4. Different from the positively charged NF membrane mentioned above, the separation performance of this composite NF membrane was mainly in connection with the change of monomer content in the aqueous phase rather than that in the organic phase, which resulted from the predomination of DDAP during its reaction with TMC caused by its low reaction activity. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Electrodialysis(ED)isamembrane-basedwatertreatmentprocess, which is generally used for ions separation through ion-selective membrane under the force of an electric fi eld [84 -86]. Electrodialysis technology has wide applications, such as desalination of saline water [87], treatment of industrial ef fl uents [88], food and chemical industries [89], pharmaceutical and biochemical industries [90], production of ultrapure water [87], concentration of RO brines [87], etc. Recently, ED equipped with monovalent selective ion exchange membranes (S-ED) has attracted a great attention on the lithium extraction from brines in terms of its economic and technical advantages. Several researches have been conducted and the relative mechanisms as well as various impact factors have been studied systematically. The ED technologies studied in the last fi ve years are shown in Table 2.

Electrodialysis technology has wide applications, such as desalination of saline water [87], treatment of industrial ef fl uents [88], food and chemical industries [89], pharmaceutical and biochemical industries [90], production of ultrapure water [87], concentration of RO brines [87], etc. Recently, ED equipped with monovalent selective ion exchange membranes (S-ED) has attracted a great attention on the lithium extraction from brines in terms of its economic and technical advantages. Several researches have been conducted and the relative mechanisms as well as various impact factors have been studied systematically. The ED technologies studied in the last fi ve years are shown in Table 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Generally, the S-ED experiments were conducted in an electrodialysis stack, which was comprised alternately placed a cation exchange membrane (CEM) and an anion exchange membrane (AEM), anode electrode (like the iridium-plated titanium electrode), and cathode electrode (such as stainless steel electrode) [43]. The dilute (feed) compartment and the concentrate (puri fi ed solution) compartment were alternately distributed on both sides of the membranes [84]. The diagram of ED set-up is shown in Fig. 5. Monovalent ion-exchange membrane was commonly used in the lithium extraction from brines. During the process, the Li + could permeate the monovalent ion-exchange membrane from brine (feed) into the concentrate (puri fi ed solution) under the electric driving force, while other ions like Mg 2+ and Ca 2+ hardly permeate the monovalent ion-exchange membrane and retain in the feed. Therefore, ED shows great potential in lithium extraction and especially the separation of lithium from magnesium in brines. Various monovalent ionexchange membranes have been studied and the in fl uence factors as well as relative mechanisms have been investigated systematically, such as temperature, fl ow velocity, current density, applied voltage, the initial volume of the product stream, and co-ions such as Na + , K + , and Ca 2+ .

texts.orig: (AEM) were investigated for lithium extraction from simulated salt-lake brines with high Mg/Li mass ratio [43]. Better results were obtained in simulated binary brine system under the optimal condition, with Li + recovery of 95.3% and the Mg/Li mass ratio decreasing from 150 to 8. It was found in the binary cations system that the increase of Mg/Li mass ratio of the feed decreased both Li + recovery and leak rate of Mg 2+ , probably since the electric double layer (EDL) was formed at the ion-exchange membrane -solution interface (Fig. 6a) [43]. The CEM consists of porous structure and fi xed charged groups (sulfonic acid radicals). According to the ionexchange isotherm of a Selemion CSO membrane at 25  C (Fig. 6b), the divalent counter-ions (Mg 2+ ) were preferentially adsorbed by CEM even without the effect of an electric fi eld, and thought to be migrated across the CEM slowly [96]. Therefore, a positive electric layer could be formed at the membrane -solution interface by these accumulated Mg 2+ ions. As the positive charge density of Li + is lower than that of Mg 2+ , leading to the weaker electrostatic repulsion to Li + than to Mg 2+ . Hence, the Li + could penetrate the positive electric layer easily than Mg 2+ . When the Mg/Li mass ratio increased, more Mg 2+ were accumulated at the interface, increasing positive charge density and improving the steric hindrance effect [96]. Therefore, both Li + recovery and leak rate of Mg 2+ suffer from a decrease.

texts.orig: lithium migration and recovery to some extent. The experimental results in the temperature range from 10  C to 30  C showed that raising temperature was conducive to improve the lithium recovery while the effect of temperature on the separation coef fi cient of lithium and coexist ions was not signi fi cant at the same lithium recovery rate [95]. This phenomenon could be explained by the expansion of macromolecular layer in cation exchange membrane and ionic diffusion (see Fig. 7). The increase of temperature induces the swelling of high polymer chains in the membrane [97,98] and thus the multivalent cations suffer from strong electrostatic repulsion owing to their larger hydrated ionic radius (Mg 2+ (0.428 nm) > Ca 2+ (0.412 nm) > Li + (0.382 nm) > Na + (0.358 nm) > K + (0.331) [99,100]). Furthermore, many X-ray and neutron diffraction studies has proved that hydrated number of ions decreases with temperature [101,102], causing the decrease of hydrated ionic radius of M n+ (M = Li, Na, K, Mg, Ca) and thus strongly affecting the ionic migration. Therefore, a larger amount of lithium could migrate through the membrane under higher temperature conditions, and then causing greater value of separation coef fi cient of Mg and Ca to Li. Similar phenomena were found in the Arrhenius plots [95]. However, higher temperature may have a negative impact on the service life of membrane. Moreover, the lower conductivity of the feed solution under the low temperature is bene fi cial to the increase of the potential gradient and thus strengthen the permeation of Li + [43]. Therefore, an appropriately low temperature in the range of 15 -20  C is conducive to the separation of lithium from magnesium.

The permeate sequence of various cations through the membrane is K + > Na + > Li + > Ca 2+ > Mg 2+ , which is in contrast to the hydrated ionic radius sequence, proving that steric hindrance played an important role in partitioning of cationic species in the feed solution. It is illustrated that monovalent ions like Na + and K + would cause obvious competitive permeation with Li + . texts.orig: The great merit of the Selemion CSO membrane is the electric double layer formed by Mg 2+ and sulfonic acid radicals at the membrane -solution interface, which helps to hinder Mg 2+ from passing through the membrane. Several in fl uence factors, such as temperature, concentration, and co-ions, have important effects on the Li + /Mg 2+ separation and should be paid more attention during the operation. In addition, both Li recovery and Mg rejection suffer from adverse impact under the condition of high Mg/Li mass ratio. And the industrial application of this Selemion CSO membrane would be limited. Therefore, the Li/Mg separation in brines with high Mg/Li mass ratio should be attached great importance in the future.

texts.orig: demonstrated that the effect of the applied voltage was similar to that of current density mentioned previously. Using higher applied voltage helped to improve the Li + migration rate, separation ef fi ciency, and Li recovery rate. But the higher the applied voltage, the greater the value of speci fi c energy consumption. As for the linear fl ow velocity, its increase was conducive to decreasing the boundary layer thickness of membrane surface and speeding up the Li + migration rate [103]. But the excessive fl ow velocity would cause mechanical damage to membranes and additional burden on the pumps. The results showed that the Li recovery rate obtained under Mg/Li mass ratio of 5 was lower than that obtained under others. According to the electro-migration and mass-transfer theory [104], during the ED process, the ion transference number in membrane phase was greater than in the solution phase, resulting in the ion de fi cit at the membrane -solution interface. Under constant voltage, the current density in membrane phase decreased, which was not conducive to Li + migration. Therefore, appropriate addition of Mg could help to improve the Li recovery. The appropriate raw solution pH of approximately 4 -5 had a positive effect on the Li recovery. Since the import of the moderate number of H + was conducive to improve the current density and mass transfer. However, overmuch H + would cause competition with Li + . Therefore, lower pH ( < 3) negatively in fl uenced the Li recovery, with high energy consumption. Under the optimal condition of an applied voltage of 5 V, a linear feed velocity of 6.2 cm/s, and pH of 4 -5, 72.46% Li was recovered, with Mg/Li ratio decreased from 60 to 7, and speci fi c energy consumption of about 0.21 kW h/mol Li + .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An electro-electrodialysis stack with Neosepta BP-1 bipolar membrane (EEDBM) and Neosepta CMX cation exchange membrane was installed to produce LiOH using Li2CO3 precipitates [92]. The fl owsheet and con fi guration are shown in Fig. 8, where CED was used to preconcentrate the low concentration simulated brine and Li2CO3 powder with a high purity of 98% was obtained. The bipolar membrane consisted of a cation exchange layer, an anion exchange layer, and a thin hydrophilic polymer interlayer between them. It could dissociate water into H + and OH � under bias of potential gradient, which could react with Li2CO3 to produce LiOH. The effects of current density and feed concentration on the EEDBM process for LiOH production were studied in terms of recovery and energy consumption. It is shown that high current density increased the LiOH concentration in that it enhanced the water dissociation. But the energy consumption also increased. As for the effect of feed concentration, it is demonstrated that the increase of Li2CO3 concentration in feed and acid compartments could lead to a high LiOH concentration in the base-2 compartment. Moreover, increasing feed concentration was conducive to reduce energy consumption, as a result of the decrease of the voltage drop across stack [105]. A LiOH solution with a high purity of 95% could be obtained under the optimal condition of a current density of 30 mA/cm 2 and a feed concentration of 0.18 M, with low energy consumption of 7.94 kW h/kg LiOH.

texts.orig: There are also several other ED techniques investigated for lithium recovery. A SPES/ion sieve composite membrane (SPES -HMON membrane) was fabricated and installed in ED process for lithium extraction [37]. It is demonstrated that the removal ef fi ciency of SPES -HMON membrane was higher than SPES membrane. However, the Li removal and Mg removal of SPES -HMON membrane were 4.32 mmol/3 h and 3.42 mmol/3 h respectively. The difference between was a little small and thus such types of membranes may only be used for selective Li recovery from second-hand Li batteries. Another ED method with a DuPont NAFION-117 cation-exchange membrane was utilized to separate Li from P and the P/Li mass ratio decreased from 1.48 (initial anolyte) to 0.23 (catholyte), with the speci fi c energy consumption of only 0.027 kW h/g Li + [93].

texts.orig: Besides, an innovative membrane electrodialysis method containing ventilation, agitation and fi ltering functions was designed for directly producing pure Li2CO3 crystals using LiCl solution and CO2 according to Eq. ([106]) [106]. The effects of LiCl concentration on the microscopic morphology and microstructure of Li2CO3 crystals were systematically investigated using particle size distribution analysis and SEM. It is found that the microcrystals change from lamellar to needle-like and then to smaller globular granules with LiCl concentration increased from 50 to 400 g/L. This method may provide a new direction for cleaner production of Li2CO3 products. Since no chemicals would be added in the production process. In the meantime, the hydrogen could probably be collected for other utilization.

texts.orig: However, the salt-lake brine system is a complex aqueous system with strong total ionic strength and charge effect. Several general principles mentioned above are in relatively simple electrolyte-solution systems and probably not suitable for the actual brine system. Therefore, lithium extraction from various feed characteristics was investigated using a constant-voltage ED, which consumed less energy than constant-current ED, and the relative mechanism was studied by thermodynamic analysis of ionic species distribution in solution and membrane property [41]. As mentioned previously, in a simple aqueous system, the permeation of ions is related to the hydrated ionic radius. While in the high-concentration aqueous solution system, the multiple forms of each ion species should be considered as it played an important role in mass transfer and in fl uenced the steric hindrance and charge effect. Mg has two species of Mg 2+ and MgCl + . MgCl + has a larger radius and lower positive charge density than Mg 2+ , leading to stronger steric hinderance and weaker electro-migration respectively. Moreover, the proportion of the MgCl + ion pair increased with the growth of Mg/Li ratio of the feed. While the Li + was always the dominant lithium specie. In addition, the Donnan effect generated stronger attraction to divalent counter-ions than monovalent counter-ions. With the increase of Mg/Li mass ratio in feed, the negative charge density within the CEM decreased by improving the shielding effect, weakening the Donnan effect. Therefore, different from the simple binary system, a relatively high Mg/Li ratio in feed was conducive to the separation selectivity of Li + /Mg 2+ in the actual complex brine system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The in fl uences of other ions were also investigated, such as Na + , K + and SO4 2-. The results showed that the percentages of free hydrated ions for both Li and Na were always dominant and decreased with a rising Na/Li ratio in feed. Therefore, the permselectivity of Li + /Na + decreased linearly with the growth of feed Na/Li ratio and the Na + would have a strong competitive permeation to Li + . The transport phenomena of K + was similar to that of Na + and the negative in fl uence of K + on Li + migration was stronger than Na + . In addition, sulfate ions hardly in fl uenced the Li recovery. And it was found that the addition of sulfate ions could be conducive to the Li transfer. Results showed that the hydrated free ions percentage of Li, Mg, and Cl increased after adding sulfate. As the sulfate ions were strongly hindered by the anion exchange membrane while Cl � could permeate easily as a result of lower steric hindrance effect [107]. The Cl � concentration in the product solution increased, improving lithium transfer as a function of the principle of electroneutrality of the solution.

texts.orig: When the constant-voltage ED with a Selemion CSO cationexchange membrane was used for lithium extraction in EastTaijiner old brine with Mg/Li mass ratio of 20.7, 90.5% Li was recovered and the Mg/Li mass ratio decreased to 2.07 under the optimum condition, with energy consumption of 0.0315 kW h/mol Li + [41]. Compared to NF [42,48], ED showed excellent performance in lithium extraction in terms of Li recovery, separation selectivity, and energy consumption. Moreover, to some extent, increasing Mg/Li mass ratio in actual brines have a positive effect on the Li recovery. Therefore, ED with this Selemion CSO membrane has a wide adaptability for lithium extraction from salt-lake brines with various feed characteristics and there are tendencies for it to be utilized in an industrial scale. In addition, as the Na + and K + have negative impacts on the Li migration and recovery, pretreatment before Li/Mg separation for removal of Na + and K + is required.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A S-ED with ion exchange membranes manufactured from Japanese ASTOM company was used to extract lithium from concentrated seawater and salt-lake brines and several important in fl uence factors were studied in detail, such as co-ions, applied voltage, and VC/VD (initial volume ratio of concentrating and desalting solution) [94]. The in fl uences of coexisting monovalent ions and anion ions on the separation of Li and Mg were researched, such as Na + , K + , SO4 2-, and HCO3 2[49]. As mentioned previously, higher concentrations of Na + and K + would cause stronger competition to the Li + migration and have negative effects on the separation of Li from Mg. This has been explained from microcosmic perspective using the partial dehydration conceptual model based on charge capillary column theory and ionic potential, where hydration potential was associated with the Z 2 /r value and used to describe the transmembrane migration process of M n+ (Z = ionic charge and r = ionic radius) [50]. However, SO4 2could improve the Li + transfer and enhance the separation of Mg/Li. In terms of the HCO3 2-, since its hydrolysis wasstrongerthanionization. The pH of the feed solution increasing with the addition of HCO3 2-, causing the generation of MgHCO3 + [108,109]. On the one hand, the amount of free hydrated Mg 2+ decreased, and on the other hand, it is dif fi cult to migrate MgHCO3 + by electrical driving force as a result of its large steric hindrance. The Mg 2+ migration was hindered to some extent and the Li + migration was relatively enhanced. Therefore, the existence of HCO3 2- had a positive effect on the Mg/Li separation.

texts.orig: decrease fi rst, followed by a growth. Opposite trend occurred on the speci fi c energy consumption. This is because at lower voltage, fewer ions were migrated to the membrane interface, leading to weak resistance and in fl uence on Li migration. And then, the increasing voltage made more ions migrate to the membrane interface and thus resistance was improved, resulting in lower current ef fi ciency and higher energy consumption. But the Li resistance could be excessed by the driving force at higher voltage, leading to a higher current ef fi ciency and lower energy consumption. As for the effect of VC/VD, it is demonstrated that higher VC/VD would lead to higher Li recovery, higher current ef fi ciency and lower speci fi c energy consumption as result of the slighter in fl uence of concentration diffusion. Under the optimum condition of a voltage of 7 V and a VC/VD of 0.6, Li recovery of 62.70% could be obtained and the Mg/Li mass ratio decreased from 16.07 to 5.62, with energy consumption of about 1.5 kW h/ mol Li.

texts.orig: Three salt-lake brine systems were also studied, including East Taijinar brine, West Taijinar brine, and Yiliping brine, with Mg/Li mass ratio of 35.18, 54.30, and 72.50 respectively. It is shown that the applied voltage positively in fl uenced the ionic driving force and Li recovery, which was the same as the concentrated seawater system. But the effect of the applied voltage on the current ef fi ciency and energy consumption was on the contrary to the concentrated seawater system. The current ef fi ciency increased fi rstly and then decreased with the growth of voltage. This is probably because the concentration of all ions in the salt-lake brines were considerably larger than the concentrated seawater system (see Table 4). The Li extraction ef fi ciency in three brine systems were compared and the order was East Taijinar > Yiliping > West Taijinar, which probably attributed to the competition of co-ions. But the Mg/Li mass ratio in East Taijinar brine, West Taijinar brine, and Yiliping brine could decrease to 3.91, 9.13, 16.97 respectively and under the optimal condition, 76.45% Li could be recovered, with energy consumption of 0.66 kW h/mol Li. The results showed that the S-ED process with membrane from ASTOM company was a potential and effective approach for lithium extraction from concentrated seawater and salt-lake brines with high Mg/Li mass ratio from economic and technical perspectives.

texts.orig: Forward osmosis (FO) technology was newly developed and several FO membranes were investigated in terms of their applications in lithium enrichment from salt-lake brine, such as cellulose triacetate on radio-frequency-weldable non-woven support (CTA-NW) and thin- fi lm composite with embedded polyester screen support (TFC-ES) [110]. The concentration factors of Li + and Mg 2+ reached 2.3 and 2.8 respectively, when the active layer of CTA-NW faced the brine orientation, with MgCl2 solution as the draw solution. However, the FO membrane showed no separation performance. Considering problems caused by the diluted nature of brine and its inherent merits, such as low membrane fouling, low energy expenditure, simple con fi guration and equipment [117 -119], it may have potential applications in brine concentration and thus enabled the applications of various treatment alternatives, such as crystallization of lithium carbonate microcrystals [120]. In addition, an XLE low pressure reverse osmosis (LPRO) membrane was investigated to separate lithium from the Chott Djerid (Tunisia) brine [39]. But it is found that the XLE LPRO membrane was inferior to the NF90 membrane no matter in terms of hydraulic permeability or selectivity between monovalent ions and surface roughness.

It is indicated from the high degree of fi tting towards the Langmuir isotherm model and the pseudo-second-order kinetics model that the monolayer and homogeneous Li + -recognition sites were distributed on the surfaces of the membranes and the adsorption on membranes contains both physical diffusion and chemisorption simultaneously [129,130]. More importantly, this membrane exhibited superior regeneration performance and high stability, with the initial rebinding capacities reaching up to 90.3% after 5 cycles of adsorption/desorption and slightly declining to 88.1% after another 5 cycles a month later. Considering its excellent and stable Na + /Li + and K + /Li + separation performance and the adverse impacts of Na + and K + on the Li/Mg separation, the Li + -imprinted membranes (Li-IIMs) could probably be used for pretreatment before Li/Mg separation using nanofi ltration or electrodialysis approaches.

texts.orig: Another metal -organic framework membrane also exhibited high selectivity of Li/Na and Li/K, as wells as Li/Mg. This negatively charged MOF membrane PSS@HKUST-1-6.7 was constructed by utilizing polystyrene sulfonate (PSS) threading HKUST-1 MOF membranes through a con fi nement conversion process [112]. It is the different hydrated ion size diameters of Li + , Na + , K + , and Mg 2+ (0.76, 0.72, 0.66, and 0.86 respectively), namely the size sieving effect, and their different binding af fi nity to the sulfonate groups (1.0, 1.98, 2.90, and 3.18 respectively) that play important roles for the high selective factor of Li + /Na + (35), Li + /K + (67), and Li + /Mg 2+ (1815) [131]. The ions separation mechanism of this MOF membrane shares a similarity with the Selemion CSO membrane, which has sulfonic groups in the polyamide layer for Li/Mg separation. In addition, the PSS@HKUST-1-6.7 membrane have several advantages, such as low activation energy of 0.21 eV, fast Li + ion fl ux and high stability. This excellent performance makes it a promising membrane approach for lithium extraction from salt-lake brines. But the current work focused only on the binary system. Therefore, the applications of PSS@HKUST-1-6.7 membrane in complex brines systems should be given more attention in the further studies.

texts.orig: Recently, a new technology (denoted as MSCDI) based on the membrane capacitive deionization (MCDI) incorporated with monovalent selective cation exchange membrane (CIMS) was designed for separating lithium from magnesium in brines [115]. The experimental setup and schematic are shown in Fig. 11. In this MSCDI process, a constant voltage was applied across the two electrodes, after that, the cations and anions in the feed will move towards the cathode and the anode respectively [151]. At the cathode area, Li + could migrate through the CIMS and then adsorb and temporarily fi x on the cathode. While Mg 2+ was blocked and therefore left in the feed solution. Several impact factors were investigated, such as cation composition, fl ow rate and concentration of feed water, applied voltage, and operating time. And the optimal conditions were the voltage of 1.0 V, fl ow rate of 30 mL/ min, and operating time of 10 min, with a selectivity coef fi cient of 2.95, Li + removal of approximately 10% and Mg 2+ removal rate of about 3%. It is found in the large modules test that the increase of feed water concentration is helped to improve the removal rate of Li + and Mg 2+ to reach 38.4% and 19.2%, respectively. And the energy consumption of large module was lower than small module, with 0.0018 and 0.0023 kW h/mol Li respectively, making the MSCDI technology have a potential for industrial application. However, the high molar ratio of Mg/Li had a negative impact on the separation performance of MSCDI.

texts.orig: A solar-powered electrolysis-based technology for lithium extraction was designed using the Li1+xAlyGe2-y(PO4)3 (LAGP) solid-state electrolyte (a kind of lithium superionic conductor (NASICON)-type solid-state electrolyte) as the lithium-ion-selective membrane [116]. The schematic and reaction mechanism are shown in Fig. 12. A LiClO4-propylene carbonate solution was employed in the cathode side to create a proton-free compartment. Under the action of the electric fi eld and good selectivity of LAGP membrane [152], only lithium could transport to the cathode and reduced to metallic lithium on the copper coil electrode while other cations were blocked and left in the anode side. XPS and XRD were conducted to characterize the products deposited on the copper foil. The results demonstrated the presence of metallic lithium on copper foil after electrolysis process. Moreover, various currents were applied to the device and it is found that the best result, a lithium production rate of 5.7 mg/(dm 2 h), could be obtained at a current density of 240 mA/cm 2 , with the operation time of 1 h and a copper foil square of 1 cm 2 . Considering the unusually worse results at 320 mA/cm 2 (a lithium production rate of about 1.5 mg/(dm 2 h)) and possible side reaction under high current density, further investigations are required to explain the in fl uence of current density on the lithium extraction. texts.orig: The great advantages of the solid electrolyte electrolysis-based technology include simple process, low energy consumption, and eco-friendly characteristic. Moreover, owing to the high Li permselectivity of solid electrolyte membrane, the adverse effects of Na + and K + on the Li + /Mg 2+ separation could be gotten rid of. This is superior to nanofi ltration and general electrodialysis, where Na + and K + may have adverse impacts on the Li + migration and Li + /Mg 2+ separation. However, the stability of separation performance is not mentioned in this work. Moreover, this work only focus on the lithium extraction from seawater. As is known, the salt-lake brines have higher ions concentration than seawater and the ions concentration may have an important in fl uence on the lithium extraction. Therefore, more deeply research should be conducted on this promising approach.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Conclusion and perspectives
texts.text: Conclusion and perspectives

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane technology represents a rapidly growing and new kind of cost-effective green research area for ionic separation, with signi fi cant progress and wide applications in the last decades. Particularly, it is an attractive approach for Li recovery from aqueous solutions. This to some extent results from its excellent lithium selectivity, low energy consumption, and environmentally friendly features. In addition, the Li extraction process has excellent safety, reliability, and high ef fi ciency compared to other conventional approaches such as precipitation and solvent extraction. It is anticipated that there is a growing tendency for developing membrane technologies to extract lithium from saltlake brines and seawater, especially those with high Mg/Li mass ratio. Since the land Li resources as spodumene and brines with low Mg/Li mass ratio have a diminishing trend. Such developments will undoubtedly relieve the market pressure for Li resources across the world.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Generally, the nanofi ltration membrane used in lithium extraction is negatively charged, such as the representative NF90 series membrane. It showed great performance for rejection of anions but the poor performance for rejection of positive ions as a function of Donnan exclusion theory. Therefore, several positively charged nanofi ltration membrane are developed in recent years via the interfacial polymerization method, such as PEI/ TMC composite NF membrane, DAPP/TMC composite NF membrane, and EDTA modifi ed composite NF membrane. High Mg 2+ rejection and low Li + rejection could be obtained, leading to better separation ef fi ciency of Li from Mg. The relevant mechanisms as well as the properties and structure of membrane were studied systematically using SEM, AFM, TEM, FIIR, and TGA. Based on these investigations, several novel nanofi ltration membranes could probably be fabricated in the future.
texts.text: Generally, the nanofi ltration membrane used in lithium extraction is negatively charged, such as the representative NF90 series membrane. It showed great performance for rejection of anions but the poor performance for rejection of positive ions as a function of Donnan exclusion theory. Therefore, several positively charged nanofi ltration membrane are developed in recent years via the interfacial polymerization method, such as PEI/ TMC composite NF membrane, DAPP/TMC composite NF membrane, and EDTA modifi ed composite NF membrane. High Mg 2+ rejection and low Li + rejection could be obtained, leading to better separation ef fi ciency of Li from Mg. The relevant mechanisms as well as the properties and structure of membrane were studied systematically using SEM, AFM, TEM, FIIR, and TGA. Based on these investigations, several novel nanofi ltration membranes could probably be fabricated in the future.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Besides, electrodialysis technology has attracted great interest in lithium extraction from brines and seawater as a result of its several merits such as high separation ef fi ciency, low energy consumption, and green process. As is driven by an electric fi eld, it showed a fast and ef fi cient separation process compared to the pressure-driven nanofi ltration membrane technology. Several cation selective membranes have been used in ED stacks, such as Selemion CSO membrane, membrane from ASTOM company, Neosepta BP-1 bipolar membrane, and SPES-HMON membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Moreover, the factors in fl uencing the separation of Li from Mg were investigated systematically, such as applied voltage, VC/VD, and coexisting ions. It is demonstrated appropriate applied voltage and VC/VD are conducive to separate Li from Mg. Considering the actual brines and seawater are a complex saline solution system, with strong total ionic strength and charge effect. The coexisting ions have an important impact on the Li separation from Mg, such as Na + , K + , Ca 2+ , SO4 2+ , and HCO3 � . In general, coexisting monovalent cations show greater in fl uence on the migration of Li + than that of Mg 2+ . And the in fl uence of coexisting anions mainly affects the migration of Mg 2+ , next Li + . Nonetheless, the electrodialysis technology showed better performance than nanofi ltration membrane technology in terms of separation coef fi cient and energy consumption. Additionally, other novel membrane technologies such as membrane capacitive deionization, solid electrolyte electrolysis-based technology also exhibit great potential for lithium extraction from brines and seawater.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Electrodialysis technologies with electric driving force and without addition of chemicals for lithium recovery from aqueous solutions with high Mg/Li mass ratios are advocated. Perhaps, developing novel monovalent selective membrane and avoiding adverse effects of coexisting ions are one of the biggest challenges encountered currently in transitioning electrodialysis technologies to industrial-scale applications. Noteworthy, nanofi ltration membrane and solid electrolyte membrane exhibit great performances for lithium extraction and could probably be combined with electrodialysis technology. Therefore, future investigation should be attacked more attention on the fabrication of novel selective membrane for electrodialysis technology and in fl uence of coexisting ions for industrial applications.
